subsections.title: Generics Fundamentals
subsections.keyRule: Generics provide compile-time type safety. List<String> guarantees only Strings — no cast needed, no ClassCastException at runtime. Type parameter erased at runtime (type erasure) — at runtime List<String> = List<Object>.

terms.what: At runtime, Java removes all generic type information — List<String> becomes List. This is 'type erasure'. Generic types only exist at compile time.
terms.why: Generics were added in Java 5 for backward compatibility. Old code using raw List still works. JVM does not need to know generic type at runtime.
terms.how: List<String> and List<Integer> are the same class at runtime — List.class. instanceof List<String> is impossible. Can only check instanceof List.
terms.where: Everywhere generics are used. Affects: reflection (cannot get generic type via reflection easily), varargs with generics, overloading.
terms.when: Type erasure matters when: using reflection to inspect types, creating generic arrays, overloading methods that differ only by generic type.
terms.issue: Cannot create a generic array: new T[] is illegal. Cannot do instanceof T. Cannot catch a generic exception type.
terms.fix: Pass Class<T> as a parameter when you need the type at runtime: <T> T fromJson(String json, Class<T> type). Jackson and Gson use this pattern.

star.s: Runtime ClassCastException when deserializing generic type.
star.a: Passed Class<T> to deserializer method. Used cls.cast() for safe conversion.

terms.what: Wildcards are flexible type bounds in generics. '? extends T' accepts T and any subclass. '? super T' accepts T and any superclass.
terms.why: Without wildcards, List<Animal> cannot accept a List<Dog> even though Dog IS-A Animal — generic types are invariant. Wildcards add flexibility.
terms.how: ? extends T (upper bound) = read-only — you can read T but cannot add. ? super T (lower bound) = write-friendly — you can add T.
terms.where: Collection APIs, utility methods: Collections.copy(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends T> src).
terms.when: Remember PECS: Producer Extends, Consumer Super. If a list PRODUCES items for you to read — use extends. If it CONSUMES items you write — use super.
terms.issue: Using List<? extends T> and trying to add to it — compilation error. Wildcards with extends are read-only.

terms.what: <T extends Comparable<T>> means T must implement Comparable. This lets you call compareTo() on T inside a generic method.
terms.why: Without bounds, you can only call Object methods on T (equals, toString). Bounds unlock the methods of the bound type.
terms.how: public <T extends Number> double sum(List<T> list) { return list.stream().mapToDouble(Number::doubleValue).sum(); } — works for List<Integer>, List<Double>, etc.
terms.where: Sorting utilities, math utilities, any generic method needing specific capabilities from T.
terms.when: Add extends bound when your generic code needs to call methods on T beyond what Object provides.
terms.issue: Multiple bounds with two classes fail: <T extends A & B> only works when B is an interface. Cannot extend two classes.
terms.fix: <T extends Serializable & Comparable<T>> is valid (Serializable is interface). Cannot have two class bounds.

star.s: Generic sort method breaks at compile time on T.
star.a: Changed to <T extends Comparable<T>> — T can now call compareTo().
star.r: Compiles and works for any Comparable type.

subsections.title: Generics Deep Dive
subsections.keyRule: Raw type = no type parameter — avoid in new code. Reifiable type = fully known at runtime. Non- reifiable type = erased at runtime (most generics). Unbounded wildcard <?> = any type, read-only. @SuppressWarnings('unchecked') — use sparingly with explanation.

terms.what: A raw type is a generic class or interface used without a type parameter. Example: List instead of List<String>. Existed before generics in Java 1.4.
terms.why: Raw types are allowed only for backward compatibility with pre-Java 5 code. They bypass type safety — compiler gives unchecked warnings.
terms.how: List list = new ArrayList(); // raw type. list.add('hello'); list.add(42); // no error — dangerous. String s = (String) list.get(1); // ClassCastException at runtime.
terms.where: Legacy codebases. Never in new code.
terms.when: Never use raw types in new code. Always parameterize: List<String>, Map<String,Integer>.
terms.issue: Raw types suppress all type checking. ClassCastException at runtime instead of compile error. Hard to debug.
terms.fix: Add type parameters everywhere. Use @SuppressWarnings('unchecked') only when you can prove type safety yourself.

star.s: Legacy code has raw type List — ClassCastException in production.
star.a: Added proper type parameter List<Customer>. Compiler caught 3 wrong casts.

terms.what: Reifiable type: fully known at runtime — primitives, raw types, non-generic classes, unbound wildcards (List<?>). Non-reifiable: type info erased at runtime — most generics like List<String>.
terms.why: Because of type erasure, JVM cannot tell List<String> from List<Integer> at runtime. This limits what you can do with generics.

terms.what: <?> means 'list of some unknown type'. You can read from it as Object. You cannot add anything (except null). Used when the type doesn't matter.
terms.why: When your method just needs to iterate and read, using List<?> is more flexible than List<Object> — List<String> IS a List<?> but is NOT a List<Object>.
terms.how: void printAll(List<?> list) { for(Object o : list) { System.out.println(o); } } — accepts any List. Cannot call list.add('x') — compile error.
terms.where: Read-only operations on collections of unknown type. Utility methods that only inspect elements.
terms.when: Use <?> when reading any collection without caring about the type. Use <T> when you need to know and use the type.
terms.issue: Cannot add to a List<?> (except null) — compile error. Attempting to add causes confusion for beginners.
terms.fix: If you need to add elements, use List<T> with a bounded type parameter. List<?> is read-only.

star.s: Utility method that prints any list.
star.a: void printAll(List<?> list) — accepts List<String>, List<Integer>, all lists.
star.r: Most flexible signature. No casting required.

terms.what: @SuppressWarnings('unchecked') suppresses compiler warnings about unchecked generic type operations. Used when you know the cast is safe but the compiler cannot verify it.
terms.why: Sometimes you must do unsafe casts for generic code — like (T[]) new Object[n]. The warning is valid but unavoidable in those cases.
terms.how: @SuppressWarnings('unchecked') T[] arr = (T[]) new Object[size]; — suppress warning on this line only.
terms.where: Generic array creation, legacy API integration, type token patterns.
terms.when: Use only when you have manually verified type safety. NEVER use it to silence warnings you don't understand.
terms.issue: Applying @SuppressWarnings at class or method level masks all unchecked warnings — real bugs hidden.
terms.fix: Apply @SuppressWarnings at the narrowest possible scope (single line or statement). Always add a comment explaining why the cast is safe.

star.a: Moved to exact unchecked lines. Added comment explaining why safe.
star.r: Only intentional suppressions. Actual type bugs now visible.

terms.what: Java arrays are covariant (String[] IS-A Object[]) but unsafe — you can assign a String[] to Object[] and put an Integer into it (ArrayStoreException). Generic collections are invariant — safer.
terms.why: This is a known Java design flaw from pre-generics era. Arrays check type at runtime (covariant + runtime check). Generics check at compile time (invariant).
